news_metadata.title: Ford Inaugurates $2.5B EV Manufacturing Complex in Mexico, Secures Multi-Year Supply Deals
news_metadata.content:
RAMOS ARISPE, Mexico (AP) — Ford Motor Co. formally inaugurated its $2.5 billion electric vehicle manufacturing complex in northern Mexico on Wednesday, marking the automaker’s largest nearshoring investment in North America to date.
The 900-acre facility will produce next-generation electric pickup trucks and SUVs for the U.S. and Mexican markets, with an annual production capacity of 250,000 EVs. The plant features fully automated stamping and welding lines, fitted with 320 industrial robotic arms, and a dedicated battery assembly workshop.
Ford also announced 10-year supply agreements with global component manufacturers to stabilize its EV supply chain, including: $1.8 billion in orders for 1200V silicon carbide power modules from onsemi; $1.2 billion in lithium iron phosphate battery cells from CATL’s new Mexico plant; and $750 million in automotive-grade MCU chips from NXP Semiconductors.
Additional local supply contracts were awarded to Mexican manufacturers, including high-voltage automotive wiring harnesses from Lear Corp, and die-cast aluminum body structural components from Nemak. The company said the localized supply chain will cut cross-border logistics costs by 18% and reduce production lead times by 12 days.
Mexico’s economy ministry said the Ford complex will create 4,600 direct jobs and drive an additional 12,000 indirect jobs in the regional auto parts supply chain.

news_metadata.title: TSMC Breaks Ground on Second Arizona Fab, Secures Local Supply of Semiconductor Materials
news_metadata.content:
PHOENIX, Ariz. (AP) — Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC) held a groundbreaking ceremony for its second advanced chip fabrication plant in Phoenix, Arizona on Thursday, doubling its planned U.S. investment to $65 billion.
The new 3nm fab, scheduled to start production in 2028, will have a monthly wafer capacity of 50,000 12-inch silicon wafers, primarily manufacturing chips for Apple, Nvidia, and AMD. TSMC’s first Arizona fab, set to start 4nm chip production this year, is already 90% complete, with 1,200 pieces of manufacturing equipment being installed.
TSMC also announced multi-year supply agreements with U.S. and European chemical manufacturers to secure critical materials for its U.S. operations, including ultra-high-purity semiconductor-grade sulfuric acid from BASF, ArF immersion photoresist from JSR Corp, and IC packaging substrates from Shinko Electric Industries.
The company has also ordered 18 high-precision wafer probe test systems from Tokyo Electron, and 24 extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography systems from ASML, to be delivered over the next three years.
U.S. Commerce Secretary said the TSMC expansion is a cornerstone of the Biden administration’s efforts to rebuild domestic semiconductor supply chains and reduce reliance on overseas chip manufacturing.

news_metadata.title: BASF Launches New Specialty Chemicals Production Line at Ludwigshafen Site, Targets Packaging and Automotive Supply Chains
news_metadata.content:
LUDWIGSHAFEN, Germany (AP) — German chemical giant BASF formally launched a new €420 million specialty chemicals production line at its headquarters Ludwigshafen Verbund site on Tuesday, expanding its capacity for high-performance polymers and industrial catalysts.
The new facility will produce 180,000 tons of food-grade polycarbonate resin annually, used for food and beverage packaging, medical device housings, and automotive interior components. A co-located production unit will also manufacture 60,000 tons of polypropylene nonwoven fabric each year, a key material for hygiene products and industrial filtration systems.
BASF also announced the startup of a new catalyst production unit, which will supply nickel-based hydrogenation catalysts to European refineries, and bio-based biodegradable polyester chips for the global packaging industry.
The company said the expansion will strengthen supply chain reliability for its European and North American customers, with 70% of the production output already locked in via 5-year supply contracts with packaging manufacturers and automotive tier-1 suppliers.
BASF’s chief operating officer said the investment is part of the company’s strategy to localize high-value chemical production and reduce reliance on long-haul imports from Asia.

news_metadata.title: COFCO Signs $3.2B Annual Grain and Protein Supply Deal with Brazilian Producers, Expands Cold Chain Fleet
news_metadata.content:
SANTOS, Brazil (AP) — China’s COFCO International has signed a $3.2 billion annual supply agreement with Brazilian agricultural producers on Friday, securing steady imports of non-GMO soybeans, corn, and frozen beef for the Chinese market.
The 12-month deal includes 2.8 million tons of non-GMO soybeans for edible oil and animal feed production, 1.2 million tons of corn, and 180,000 tons of halal frozen beef from Brazil’s southern cattle-producing states.
To support the increased trade volume, COFCO also announced a $450 million investment to expand its cold chain logistics fleet, ordering 25 new 40-foot reefer shipping containers from Maersk Container Industry, and 12 refrigerated bulk cargo vessels to be built at Chinese shipyards.
Additional supply chain investments include new grain fumigation equipment at the Port of Santos, and food-grade composite packaging film production lines at COFCO’s Brazilian packaging subsidiary, to ensure compliance with Chinese food safety import standards.
Brazil’s agriculture ministry said the deal will solidify Brazil’s position as China’s top supplier of agricultural commodities, with bilateral agricultural trade expected to exceed $60 billion in 2026.

news_metadata.title: Siemens Gamesa Secures €4.8B Offshore Wind Farm Contract in North Sea, Locks in Component Supply Chain
news_metadata.content:
COPENHAGEN, Denmark (AP) — Siemens Gamesa Renewable Energy has signed a €4.8 billion contract with Danish energy firm Ørsted on Monday, to supply and install 142 offshore wind turbines for the 2.8GW Hornsea 3 wind farm in the UK North Sea.
The contract includes 142 of Siemens Gamesa’s 14MW offshore wind turbines, each fitted with 115-meter carbon fiber wind turbine blades, a heavy-duty planetary gearbox, and a permanent magnet synchronous generator. The project is scheduled to be fully operational by 2029, and will generate enough electricity to power 3.2 million UK households.
Siemens Gamesa also announced multi-year supply agreements to stabilize its European wind turbine supply chain, including a €1.2 billion order for carbon fiber composite materials from Toray Industries, and €800 million in wind turbine tower sections from Spanish steel fabricator Windar Renovables.
Additional contracts were awarded for 220kV submarine high-voltage power cables from Nexans, and turbine condition monitoring systems from SKF. The company said the long-term supply deals will mitigate steel and composite material price volatility, and ensure on-time delivery for the project.
